args:
A 54-year-old Caucasian man presented to his infectious disease physician in October 2014 with fatigue, nausea, and anorexia for the last 5 days that had been preceded by several episodes of bloody diarrhea of 10 days' duration. He denied abdominal pain, diminished urine output, or macroscopic hematuria. His past medical history was significant for HIV infection for 10 years with the most recent CD4 count of 180; current treatment consisted of Atazanavir, Emtricitabine, Tenofovir, and Ritonavir. He also had a history of chronic hepatitis C infection. He had a previous history of injection drug use that had been complicated by 2 episodes of infective endocarditis requiring a mechanical mitral valve replacement in 1997. Since that time, he had been treated with warfarin anticoagulation, INR 2.5–3.5. He admitted taking additional doses of warfarin when he had migraine headaches as he thought this would improve blood flow. On physical examination, he was afebrile with a supine blood pressure (BP) of 109/65 mmHg but with postural drop to 95/55 mmHg. His JVP was flat with no evidence of lower extremity edema. He did not have a skin rash or detectable heart murmur. The remainder of his examination was otherwise unremarkable.
His laboratory data on admission are outlined in . His urinalysis was significant for 5–30 RBC/HPF and hyaline casts; no heme granular or red blood cell casts were seen. His urine albumin : creatinine ratio was 217 g/mol Cr. His creatinine which was 1.4 mg/dL in July 2014 had increased to 19.9 mg/dL. Abdominal ultrasonography showed normal-sized kidneys with slightly increased right renal parenchymal echogenicity.
After fluid resuscitation failed to improve his acute kidney injury (AKI), he underwent hemodialysis for metabolic acidosis. Other investigations included (1) anemia work-up for a declining hemoglobin [11.1 g/dL to 6.6 g/dL after fluid resuscitation without evidence of bleeding or falling platelet count (137 × 109/L)] included blood film review which showed polychromasia without schistocytes, LDH 153 (N: 100–205 units), haptoglobin < 0.08 (N: 0.3–2.0 units), total bilirubin of 43 (N: 3–17 units), direct bilirubin of 6 (N: 2–9 units), iron percent saturation 86 (N: 20–50), and ferritin 314 (N: 24–336), making TTP and HUS unlikely with the stable platelet count and absence of schistocytes on blood smear. Further investigations showed (1) a positive random urine for hemosiderin and (2) renal biopsy that showed severe acute tubular necrosis (ATN) caused by extensive renal hemosiderosis with extensive iron depositions in the tubules; the glomeruli were spared from deposition and damage (Figures and ). There was focal interstitial chronic inflammation, mild interstitial fibrosis, mild tubular atrophy, and mild-to-moderate arterial atherosclerosis. Immunofluorescent staining: IgG: Negative, IgA: negative, IgM: equivocal, complement (C3): equivocal, C1q: negative, albumin: negative, and Kappa/Lambda: negative. Special stains for iron show strong diffuse positivity in the tubules (). Electron microscopy showed segmental effacement of foot process with no evidence of electron dense deposits ().
(3) Clostridium difficile toxin B was detected by PCR, (4) normal genetic testing for HFE (human hemochromatosis protein gene) mutation excluding hemochromatosis, negative flow cytometry for PNH (paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria), a normal glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase screen (excluding hemolysis secondary to G6PD deficiency anemia), and (5) transesophageal echocardiogram which showed that the prosthetic mitral valve is functioning well without evidence of paravalvular leak or mitral valve regurgitation
The patient stabilized in hospital with treatment of his AKI and Clostridium difficile infection. He remained on 3-times-per-week hemodialysis for ten weeks when his AKI recovered and his central venous catheter was removed.

output.reasoning: Patient is a 54-year-old male (inferred from pronouns 'he', 'his'). For RCRI (Revised Cardiac Risk Index), conditions assessed: history of ischemic heart disease, heart failure, cerebrovascular disease, diabetes mellitus requiring insulin, preoperative serum creatinine >2 mg/dL, and high-risk surgery. No explicit mention of ischemic heart disease (e.g., MI, angina), cerebrovascular disease (e.g., stroke, TIA), or diabetes mellitus (medications like Tenofovir/Emtricitabine are for HIV, not diabetes). Patient has chronic hepatitis C and HIV, but these are not RCRI criteria. Patient has a mechanical mitral valve replacement (1997) which implies history of infective endocarditis but not ischemic heart disease; warfarin is for anticoagulation due to mechanical valve, not for cardiac ischemia. Heart failure: JVP flat, no edema, no murmur, BP 109/65 (normal), no evidence of CHF. Cerebrovascular disease: denied stroke symptoms, no history mentioned. Diabetes: no mention, no diabetic medications (e.g., metformin). Preoperative serum creatinine: not applicable as this is not a preoperative setting; current creatinine is 19.9 mg/dL (acute kidney injury), but RCRI requires preoperative creatinine >2 mg/dL for risk stratification before surgery. This note describes an admission for AKI, not preoperative evaluation. High-risk surgery: not mentioned; patient presented for infectious symptoms. Thus, all RCRI conditions are absent.

args:
A 54-year-old Caucasian man presented to his infectious disease physician in October 2014 with fatigue, nausea, and anorexia for the last 5 days that had been preceded by several episodes of bloody diarrhea of 10 days' duration. He denied abdominal pain, diminished urine output, or macroscopic hematuria. His past medical history was significant for HIV infection for 10 years with the most recent CD4 count of 180; current treatment consisted of Atazanavir, Emtricitabine, Tenofovir, and Ritonavir. He also had a history of chronic hepatitis C infection. He had a previous history of injection drug use that had been complicated by 2 episodes of infective endocarditis requiring a mechanical mitral valve replacement in 1997. Since that time, he had been treated with warfarin anticoagulation, INR 2.5–3.5. He admitted taking additional doses of warfarin when he had migraine headaches as he thought this would improve blood flow. On physical examination, he was afebrile with a supine blood pressure (BP) of 109/65 mmHg but with postural drop to 95/55 mmHg. His JVP was flat with no evidence of lower extremity edema. He did not have a skin rash or detectable heart murmur. The remainder of his examination was otherwise unremarkable.
His laboratory data on admission are outlined in . His urinalysis was significant for 5–30 RBC/HPF and hyaline casts; no heme granular or red blood cell casts were seen. His urine albumin : creatinine ratio was 217 g/mol Cr. His creatinine which was 1.4 mg/dL in July 2014 had increased to 19.9 mg/dL. Abdominal ultrasonography showed normal-sized kidneys with slightly increased right renal parenchymal echogenicity.
After fluid resuscitation failed to improve his acute kidney injury (AKI), he underwent hemodialysis for metabolic acidosis. Other investigations included (1) anemia work-up for a declining hemoglobin [11.1 g/dL to 6.6 g/dL after fluid resuscitation without evidence of bleeding or falling platelet count (137 × 109/L)] included blood film review which showed polychromasia without schistocytes, LDH 153 (N: 100–205 units), haptoglobin < 0.08 (N: 0.3–2.0 units), total bilirubin of 43 (N: 3–17 units), direct bilirubin of 6 (N: 2–9 units), iron percent saturation 86 (N: 20–50), and ferritin 314 (N: 24–336), making TTP and HUS unlikely with the stable platelet count and absence of schistocytes on blood smear. Further investigations showed (1) a positive random urine for hemosiderin and (2) renal biopsy that showed severe acute tubular necrosis (ATN) caused by extensive renal hemosiderosis with extensive iron depositions in the tubules; the glomeruli were spared from deposition and damage (Figures and ). There was focal interstitial chronic inflammation, mild interstitial fibrosis, mild tubular atrophy, and mild-to-moderate arterial atherosclerosis. Immunofluorescent staining: IgG: Negative, IgA: negative, IgM: equivocal, complement (C3): equivocal, C1q: negative, albumin: negative, and Kappa/Lambda: negative. Special stains for iron show strong diffuse positivity in the tubules (). Electron microscopy showed segmental effacement of foot process with no evidence of electron dense deposits ().
(3) Clostridium difficile toxin B was detected by PCR, (4) normal genetic testing for HFE (human hemochromatosis protein gene) mutation excluding hemochromatosis, negative flow cytometry for PNH (paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria), a normal glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase screen (excluding hemolysis secondary to G6PD deficiency anemia), and (5) transesophageal echocardiogram which showed that the prosthetic mitral valve is functioning well without evidence of paravalvular leak or mitral valve regurgitation
The patient stabilized in hospital with treatment of his AKI and Clostridium difficile infection. He remained on 3-times-per-week hemodialysis for ten weeks when his AKI recovered and his central venous catheter was removed.
high_risk_surgery, ischemic_heart_disease, congestive_heart_failure, cerebrovascular_disease, insulin_diabetes, creatinine_elevated

args:
A 47-year-old female patient presented to our emergency department with chest pain. The pain was: retrosternal; of sudden onset, starting 2 hours prior to presentation at our hospital; continuous and worsening in severity; described as pressure; irradiating to the neck and left upper extremity; had no precipitating or alleviating factors; and was associated with nausea, diaphoresis, and shortness of breath. She denied headache, dizziness, syncope, palpitations, or vomiting. Prior to the pain beginning, the patient denied having any of the symptoms. Review of her system was negative except for the symptoms mentioned.
The patient had no significant medical history except that she was seen many years prior for mild thrombocytosis during her pregnancy. At that time, she was started on aspirin. She did not keep her follow-up appointments afterward. Her surgical history was significant for hysterectomy.
Her father had had coronary artery disease and MI at the age of 50; he died at the age of 67. Her mother had been diagnosed with chronic myelogenous leukemia and succumbed to blastic crisis after 7 years. Three of the patient’s four children were positive for Factor V Leiden and one daughter developed extensive deep-vein thrombosis after 4 weeks on the oral contraceptive pills and major bleeding on warfarin (severe hemorrhagic ovarian cysts).
The patient was a nonsmoker and denied any alcohol or drug use. She had no history of allergy. Her only medication was baby aspirin once daily.
On initial examination, the patient was awake, alert, and oriented. Her blood pressure was 90/60 mm Hg, and she had a pulse of 80 beats per minute, respiratory rate of 12 breaths per minute, and a temperature of 99°F. There was no jugular vein distention. Auscultation was clear throughout both lungs. Cardiac auscultation revealed an S4 gallop. No loud murmurs were appreciated. Abdominal exam was unremarkable, with no hepatosplenomegaly. There was no edema of the lower extremities. There was positive erythromelalgia of the hands.
Her electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm and ST-segment elevation in the anterior leads from V1 to V6 consistent with a large anterior wall infarct ().
Initial blood work showed negative cardiac enzymes. Her hemoglobin was 13.7 g/dL, white blood cell count was 14.2 th/mm3, and platelet count was 935,000 th/mm3. The patient’s basic metabolic profile was normal. Her low-density lipoprotein and high-density lipoprotein were 148 mg/dL and 61 mg/dL, respectively. Here total cholestrol concentration was 229 mg/dL. 
The patient was transferred urgently to the catheterization lab. Left heart catheterization showed a total proximal left anterior descending artery (LAD) occlusion (), while her other coronary arteries were normal. A successful balloon angioplasty, with two bare metal stents, was performed on the 100% lesion in the proximal LAD, and an excellent result was attained (). The patient was started, in addition to aspirin (325 mg daily), on clopidogrel, a statin, and beta-blockers. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35% with severe hypokinesia of the mid-apical anteroseptal and apical walls.
During her hospital stay, the patient was seen by the hematology-oncology team. Further workup was done and this showed a positive JAK-2 mutation. She was negative for the Factor V Leiden mutation (R506Q) and BCR-ABL genes translocation (Philadelphia chromosome). The rest of her blood work was within normal limits, including the erythrocyte sedimentation rate (1 mm/h), ferritin (151 ng/mL), thyroid-stimulating hormone (1.3 mIU/mL), folate (10.2 ng/mL), vitamin B12 (382 pg/mL) and bleeding time (1.1 min). Patient was diagnosed with ET and started on hydroxyurea 1,000 mg twice daily. Patient was discharged in a stable condition with the plan to follow up with the cardiologist and hematologist.
Two weeks later, during a follow-up at the hematology office, her complete blood count revealed a platelet count of 803 th/mm3, so the dose of hydroxyurea was increased to 2,500 mg twice daily. Subsequent follow-up a few weeks later indicated a platelet count of 310 th/mm3, so the patient was kept on the same dose for the next 3 years. Her platelet count four months ago was still within the normal range.
The patient has been followed-up in the outpatient cardiology office for the last 3 years. Within a few months, her left ventricular function was found to have improved to normal and has been normal since then. We managed her as a coronary artery disease (CAD) equivalent, continued on dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel) beyond 1 year.

output.reasoning: Patient is a 47-year-old female. Framingham Risk Score requires age, sex, total cholesterol, HDL cholesterol, systolic BP, smoking status, and hypertension treatment. Age 47: points for age. Female: points for sex. Total cholesterol 229 mg/dL: points for high cholesterol. HDL 61 mg/dL: points for favorable HDL. BP 90/60 mm Hg: low systolic BP (no points). Not a smoker: no points. Not on hypertension medication (baby aspirin is antiplatelet, not antihypertensive): no points. No diabetes mentioned. Conditions present: age_47_female, high_cholesterol, high_ldl, low_hdl? HDL 61 is good (high HDL reduces risk), so no points for low HDL. LDL 148 mg/dL: high (points). Conditions absent: hypertension, smoking, diabetes.

The patient’s basic metabolic profile was normal. Her low-density lipoprotein and high-density lipoprotein were 148 mg/dL and 61 mg/dL, respectively. Here total cholestrol concentration was 229 mg/dL. 
The patient was transferred urgently to the catheterization lab. Left heart catheterization showed a total proximal left anterior descending artery (LAD) occlusion (), while her other coronary arteries were normal. A successful balloon angioplasty, with two bare metal stents, was performed on the 100% lesion in the proximal LAD, and an excellent result was attained (). The patient was started, in addition to aspirin (325 mg daily), on clopidogrel, a statin, and beta-blockers. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35% with severe hypokinesia of the mid-apical anteroseptal and apical walls.
During her hospital stay, the patient was seen by the hematology-oncology team. Further workup was done and this showed a positive JAK-2 mutation. She was negative for the Factor V Leiden mutation (R506Q) and BCR-ABL genes translocation (Philadelphia chromosome). The rest of her blood work was within normal limits, including the erythrocyte sedimentation rate (1 mm/h), ferritin (151 ng/mL), thyroid-stimulating hormone (1.3 mIU/mL), folate (10.2 ng/mL), vitamin B12 (382 pg/mL) and bleeding time (1.1 min). Patient was diagnosed with ET and started on hydroxyurea 1,000 mg twice daily. Patient was discharged in a stable condition with the plan to follow up with the cardiologist and hematologist.
Two weeks later, during a follow-up at the hematology office, her complete blood count revealed a platelet count of 803 th/mm3, so the dose of hydroxyurea was increased to 2,500 mg twice daily. Subsequent follow-up a few weeks later indicated a platelet count of 310 th/mm3, so the patient was kept on the same dose for the next 3 years. Her platelet count four months ago was still within the normal range.
The patient has been followed-up in the outpatient cardiology office for the last 3 years. Within a few months, her left ventricular function was found to have improved to normal and has been normal since then. We managed her as a coronary artery disease (CAD) equivalent, continued on dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel) beyond 1 year.

calculator_name: HAS-BLED Score for Major Bleeding Risk

args: What is the patient's HAS-BLED score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

args:
A 63-year-old female was admitted to our hospital for radiofrequency catheter ablation (RFCA) to treat chronic atrial fibrillation. Her past medical history included hypertension and a cerebrovascular accident 1 year before for which she had been taking warfarin, amiodarone and an angiotension receptor blocker. There was no history of diabetes, cardiac valvular disease or thyroid disease. She did not smoke or consume alcohol. She was alert and oriented. Auscultation of the neck showed normal carotid upstrokes.
The next day after admission, RFCA for chronic atrial fibrillation was successfully performed without in-lab complication. Ten hours after RFCA, she started having shortness of breath as well as painful swelling on the anterior part of her right neck and the symptoms were getting worse. Her right CCA was accidentally ruptured while attempting needle and sheath insertion to the right internal jugular vein. She was hemodynamically unstable (blood pressure 83/53 mmHg) and mechanically ventilated. Hematocrit had dropped from 37% to 26.8% in 4 hours. A computed tomogram (CT) of the neck revealed extensive hemorrhage around the right CCA which expanded into the mediastinum (). We consulted with otolaryngologist for an emergency operation. They recommended elective operation the following morning because they believed intubation to keep her airway from obstruction. However, her symptoms and vital signs deteriorated rapidly during observation. The size of subcutaneous hematoma around neck and shoulder was getting bigger and we decided emergency stenting for the perforated carotid artery was necessary. INR is within therapeutic range at 2.5. 
A 7 French (F) sheath was inserted into the right femoral artery. Heparinization was performed during the intervention with an active clotting time kept between 250 to 300 seconds. A 5F Headhunter diagnostic catheter (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) was placed in the right CCA. Angiography showed bleeding from the proximal right CCA (). A 0.035inch stiff Amplatz wire (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) was used to exchange a 7F Shuttle guide sheath (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) into the right CCA. The 7F Shuttle guide sheath (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) was positioned at the proximal right CCA. A Jostent peripheral stent graft (5×28 mm; Abbott, Abbott Park, IL, USA) was hand-mounted onto an Ultrathin diamond balloon catheter (6×40 mm; Natick, Boston Scientific, MA, USA). The stent graft mounted balloon was advanced to the injured segment of the CCA () and was positioned at the level of the injured CCA. After a control angiogram was obtained, the stent graft mounted balloon was inflated to 15 atm (). The balloon was then deflated while negative pressure was applied, and the balloon was removed. A final angiogram showed total closure of the ruptured portion of the CCA () and the patient had no neurologic changes after the procedure. She was extubated in 5 days after stenting and discharged 10 days after stenting without any complications. She was followed carefully in the outpatient department and was given aspirin and clopidogrel. Follow-up CT showed a decrease in the extent of hematoma around CCA and in the mediastinum 25 days after procedure ().

output.reasoning: Patient is a 63-year-old female. HAS-BLED Score criteria: Hypertension is explicitly mentioned in medical history. Abnormal renal function not mentioned, but warfarin use implies anticoagulation which is a bleeding risk. Stroke history present (cerebrovascular accident 1 year prior). Bleeding history not explicitly mentioned, but the current event involves major bleeding from carotid artery rupture. Labile INR: INR is 2.5 which is within therapeutic range for warfarin (not labile). Elderly: Age 63 (<65, so not elderly for this score). Drugs: Concurrent use of warfarin (anticoagulant) and aspirin/clopidogrel (antiplatelets) mentioned at discharge. Alcohol: Explicitly stated "did not consume alcohol".

A 63-year-old female was admitted to our hospital for radiofrequency catheter ablation (RFCA) to treat chronic atrial fibrillation. Her past medical history included hypertension and a cerebrovascular accident 1 year before for which she had been taking warfarin, amiodarone and an angiotension receptor blocker. There was no history of diabetes, cardiac valvular disease or thyroid disease. She did not smoke or consume alcohol. She was alert and oriented. Auscultation of the neck showed normal carotid upstrokes.
The next day after admission, RFCA for chronic atrial fibrillation was successfully performed without in-lab complication. Ten hours after RFCA, she started having shortness of breath as well as painful swelling on the anterior part of her right neck and the symptoms were getting worse. Her right CCA was accidentally ruptured while attempting needle and sheath insertion to the right internal jugular vein. She was hemodynamically unstable (blood pressure 83/53 mmHg) and mechanically ventilated. Hematocrit had dropped from 37% to 26.8% in 4 hours. A computed tomogram (CT) of the neck revealed extensive hemorrhage around the right CCA which expanded into the mediastinum (). We consulted with otolaryngologist for an emergency operation. They recommended elective operation the following morning because they believed intubation to keep her airway from obstruction. However, her symptoms and vital signs deteriorated rapidly during observation. The size of subcutaneous hematoma around neck and shoulder was getting bigger and we decided emergency stenting for the perforated carotid artery was necessary. INR is within therapeutic range at 2.5. 
A 7 French (F) sheath was inserted into the right femoral artery. Heparinization was performed during the intervention with an active clotting time kept between 250 to 300 seconds. A 5F Headhunter diagnostic catheter (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) was placed in the right CCA. Angiography showed bleeding from the proximal right CCA (). A 0.035inch stiff Amplatz wire (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) was used to exchange a 7F Shuttle guide sheath (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) into the right CCA. The 7F Shuttle guide sheath (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) was positioned at the proximal right CCA. A Jostent peripheral stent graft (5×28 mm; Abbott, Abbott Park, IL, USA) was hand-mounted onto an Ultrathin diamond balloon catheter (6×40 mm; Natick, Boston Scientific, MA, USA). The stent graft mounted balloon was advanced to the injured segment of the CCA () and was positioned at the level of the injured CCA. After a control angiogram was obtained, the stent graft mounted balloon was inflated to 15 atm (). The balloon was then deflated while negative pressure was applied, and the balloon was removed. A final angiogram showed total closure of the ruptured portion of the CCA () and the patient had no neurologic changes after the procedure. She was extubated in 5 days after stenting and discharged 10 days after stenting without any complications. She was followed carefully in the outpatient department and was given aspirin and clopidogrel. Follow-up CT showed a decrease in the extent of hematoma around CCA and in the mediastinum 25 days after procedure ().

labile_inr: Labile INR (TTR < 60%) (+1)

- Conditions PRESENT: hypertension, stroke, bleeding, drugs
- Conditions ABSENT: abnormal_renal_function, abnormal_liver_function, labile_inr, elderly, alcohol
- Demographics: Age=63, Sex=female

A 63-year-old female was admitted to our hospital for radiofrequency catheter ablation (RFCA) to treat chronic atrial fibrillation. Her past medical history included hypertension and a cerebrovascular accident 1 year before for which she had been taking warfarin, amiodarone and an angiotension receptor blocker. There was no history of diabetes, cardiac valvular disease or thyroid disease. She did not smoke or consume alcohol. She was alert and oriented. Auscultation of the neck showed normal carotid upstrokes.
The next day after admission, RFCA for chronic atrial fibrillation was successfully performed without in-lab complication. Ten hours after RFCA, she started having shortness of breath as well as painful swelling on the anterior part of her right neck and the symptoms were getting worse. Her right CCA was accidentally ruptured while attempting needle and sheath insertion to the right internal jugular vein. She was hemodynamically unstable (blood pressure 83/53 mmHg) and mechanically ventilated. Hematocrit had dropped from 37% to 26.8% in 4 hours. A computed tomogram (CT) of the neck revealed extensive hemorrhage around the right CCA which expanded into the mediastinum (). We consulted with otolaryngologist for an emergency operation. They recommended elective operation the following morning because they believed intubation to keep her airway from obstruction. However, her symptoms and vital signs deteriorated rapidly during observation. The size of subcutaneous hematoma around neck and shoulder was getting bigger and we decided emergency stenting for the perforated carotid artery was necessary. INR is within therapeutic range at 2.5. 
A 7 French (F) sheath was inserted into the right femoral artery. Heparinization was performed during the intervention with an active clotting time kept between 250 to 300 seconds. A 5F Headhunter diagnostic catheter (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) was placed in the right CCA. Angiography showed bleeding from the proximal right CCA (). A 0.035inch stiff Amplatz wire (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) was used to exchange a 7F Shuttle guide sheath (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) into the right CCA. The 7F Shuttle guide sheath (Cook, Bloomington, IN, USA) was positioned at the proximal right CCA. A Jostent peripheral stent graft (5×28 mm; Abbott, Abbott Park, IL, USA) was hand-mounted onto an Ultrathin diamond balloon catheter (6×40 mm; Natick, Boston Scientific, MA, USA). The stent graft mounted balloon was advanced to the injured segment of the CCA () and was positioned at the level of the injured CCA. After a control angiogram was obtained, the stent graft mounted balloon was inflated to 15 atm (). The balloon was then deflated while negative pressure was applied, and the balloon was removed. A final angiogram showed total closure of the ruptured portion of the CCA () and the patient had no neurologic changes after the procedure. She was extubated in 5 days after stenting and discharged 10 days after stenting without any complications. She was followed carefully in the outpatient department and was given aspirin and clopidogrel. Follow-up CT showed a decrease in the extent of hematoma around CCA and in the mediastinum 25 days after procedure ().
What is the patient's HAS-BLED score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

Consider the following documentation stub for a Python function.  Note
that this is documentation, not a full implementation.
```python
def simulate_medical_value(patient_note: str, question: str) -> float:
    """Calculate a medical value from a patient note.

    Calculate a medical value from a patient note.
    
    Given a patient note and a calculation question, reason step by step:
    1. Carefully read the patient note to extract all relevant clinical values
    2. Identify what medical calculation/score is needed
    3. Apply the appropriate formula from the reference below
    4. Show your calculation step by step, checking your arithmetic
    
    FORMULAS (use these exact formulas):
    - APACHE II Score: Acute Physiology Score + Age Points + Chronic Health Points
    - Adjusted Body Weight: ABW = IBW + 0.4 * (actual_weight - IBW)
    - Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    - Anion Gap: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    - Body Mass Index (BMI): BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2
    - Body Surface Area (Mosteller): BSA = sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600)
    - CHA2DS2-VASc Score: Sum of: CHF(+1), HTN(+1), Age>=75(+2)/65-74(+1), DM(+1), Stroke/TIA(+2), Vascular(+1), Female(+1)
    - CKD-EPI GFR (2021): 142 * min(Cr/kappa,1)^alpha * max(Cr/kappa,1)^-1.200 * 0.9938^age * sex_coef
    - CURB-65 Score: C + U + R + B + 65 (0-5 points)
    - Calcium Correction for Hypoalbuminemia: Corrected Ca = Ca + 0.8 * (4 - albumin)
    - Caprini VTE Score: Sum of weighted risk factors
    - Centor Score (McIsaac): Sum with age modifier
    - Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI): Sum of weighted comorbidity points + age points
    - Child-Pugh Score: Sum of points for bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
    - Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault): CrCl = ((140 - age) * adjusted_weight * gender_coef) / (Cr * 72)
    - Date of Conception: gestational_weeks - 2 (conception ~2 weeks after LMP)
    - Delta Gap: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    - Delta Ratio: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    - Estimated Due Date: 40 - current_gestational_age
    - FIB-4 Index: FIB-4 = (Age * AST) / (Platelets * sqrt(ALT))
    - FeverPAIN Score: Fever + Purulence + Attend rapidly + Inflamed tonsils + No cough/coryza (0-5)
    - Fractional Excretion of Sodium (FENa): FENa = (UNa * SCr) / (SNa * UCr) * 100
    - Framingham Risk Score: Complex multivariate equation based on age, sex, lipids, BP, smoking
    - Free Water Deficit: FWD = TBW% * weight * (Na/140 - 1)
    - Gestational Age: weeks + days/7
    - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): Eye + Verbal + Motor (3-15)
    - Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): Sum of clinical and lab criteria (0-23)
    - HAS-BLED Score: H-A-S-B-L-E-D criteria (0-9 points)
    - HEART Score: Sum of History + ECG + Age + Risk factors + Troponin (each 0-2 points)
    - HOMA-IR: HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405
    - Ideal Body Weight (Devine): Male: IBW = 50 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60), Female: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
    - LDL Calculated (Friedewald): LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides / 5)
    - MDRD GFR: 175 * (Cr)^-1.154 * (age)^-0.203 * sex_coef
    - MELD-Na Score: MELD = 10 * (0.957*ln(Cr) + 0.378*ln(Bili) + 1.120*ln(INR) + 0.643)
    - Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule): First 10kg: 4ml/kg/hr, Next 10kg: 2ml/kg/hr, Remaining: 1ml/kg/hr
    - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): MAP = (2 * DBP + SBP) / 3
    - Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME): Sum of (opioid dose * conversion factor)
    - PERC Rule: 8 criteria; PERC negative if all 0
    - PSI/PORT Score: Demographics + Comorbidities + Physical Exam + Labs
    - QTc (Bazett): QTc = QT / sqrt(RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Framingham): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Fridericia): QTc = QT / RR^(1/3) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Hodges): QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)
    - QTc (Rautaharju): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): Sum of 6 risk factors (0-6 points)
    - SIRS Criteria: Count of criteria met (>= 2 = SIRS positive)
    - SOFA Score: Sum of 6 organ system scores (0-24)
    - Serum Osmolality: Osm = 2*Na + BUN/2.8 + Glucose/18
    - Sodium Correction for Hyperglycemia: Corrected Na = Na + 0.024 * (glucose - 100)
    - Steroid Conversion: Prednisone equivalent = dose * conversion_factor
    - Target Weight: Target Weight = target_bmi * (height_m)^2
    - Wells' Criteria for DVT: Sum of clinical criteria points
    - Wells' Criteria for PE: Sum of weighted criteria
    
    Important:
    - Be precise with extracted values. Double-check your arithmetic.
    - For sex/gender: "man"/"male"/"he" → male, "woman"/"female"/"she" → female.
    - Convert units as needed (lbs→kg: ×0.453592, feet/inches→cm: (ft×12+in)×2.54).
    - For scoring systems: carefully check each criterion against the patient note.
      Look for conditions implied by medications or clinical findings, not just
      explicitly named conditions.
    
    Return the final numeric answer.
    
    Examples:
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 70-year-old male weighing 80 kg, height 175 cm.", "What is the patient's BMI?")
    26.122
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 65-year-old female, BP 130/85 mmHg.", "What is the patient's Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)?")
    100.0
    """
    ...

```

trace.raw_response:
To determine the patient's HAS-BLED score at the time of admission, we need to evaluate the patient note for each criterion in the HAS-BLED scoring system. The HAS-BLED score assesses bleeding risk in patients with atrial fibrillation and includes the following criteria (each worth 1 point unless specified):

- H: Hypertension (systolic BP >160 mmHg)
- A: Abnormal renal or liver function (1 point each)
- S: Stroke (history of stroke)
- B: Bleeding (history or predisposition to bleeding)
- L: Labile INRs (if on warfarin, INR unstable)
- E: Elderly (age >65 years)
- D: Drugs or alcohol (1 point for antiplatelets or NSAIDs; 1 point for alcohol abuse)

Now, let's analyze the patient note at admission:

1. Hypertension (H): The patient has a history of hypertension ("past medical history included hypertension"). At admission, the blood pressure is not recorded, but the history is present. However, the note later mentions "blood pressure 83/53 mmHg" after the complication, which is low. But at admission, we only know the history. Since hypertension is listed in the history, we should count this. But note: the criterion is "uncontrolled hypertension" (systolic >160) typically. However, the documentation stub says "HTN(+1)" for CHA2DS2-VASc, but for HAS-BLED, it is specifically "Hypertension (systolic BP >160 mmHg)". Since the history is present but no value >160 is given, we should not assume it. However, the patient is on an "angiotension receptor blocker", indicating treated hypertension. Without a high BP reading at admission, we should not assign this point. But let's be cautious: the stub says "carefully check each criterion", and "look for conditions implied by medications". The medication implies hypertension, but the criterion requires systolic >160. Since no value is given, we do not assign this point.

2. Abnormal renal or liver function (A): The note does not mention any renal or liver issues. No lab values are given for creatinine, liver enzymes, etc. So, 0 points.

3. Stroke (S): The patient has a history of "cerebrovascular accident 1 year before". This counts as a stroke. So, 1 point.

4. Bleeding (B): There is no history of bleeding or predisposition mentioned at admission. The bleeding occurred after the procedure. So, at admission, no bleeding history. So, 0 points.

5. Labile INRs (L): The patient is on warfarin. The note later says "INR is within therapeutic range at 2.5", which is stable. But at admission, we don't have INR history. However, the stub says "look for conditions implied by medications". Being on warfarin might imply risk, but the criterion is specifically "labile INRs" (unstable). Since no instability is mentioned, we should not assign this point.

6. Elderly (E): The patient is 63 years old. The criterion is age >65. So, 0 points.

7. Drugs or alcohol (D): The patient is on warfarin (which is an anticoagulant, not antiplatelet) and amiodarone. No antiplatelets (like aspirin or clopidogrel) are mentioned at admission. She is not on NSAIDs. She does not consume alcohol ("did not smoke or consume alcohol"). So, 0 points.

Therefore, the only point is for Stroke (S). So, the HAS-BLED score is 1.

However, let's double-check: the patient is on warfarin, which is a drug that increases bleeding risk, but it is not specifically listed under "Drugs" in HAS-BLED? The "Drugs" criterion is for antiplatelets or NSAIDs, not anticoagulants. Anticoagulants are considered in the overall context but are not a direct point. So, no point.

Also, note that the patient is female, but gender is not a criterion in HAS-BLED.

So, total score = 1.

Final answer: 1.0 (as a float).

args: A 53-year-old male presented with 1 day duration of acute severe right lower quadrant abdominal pain, sharp, non-radiating associated with fever, chills and nausea but no vomiting or weight loss. On physical exam, his vital signs were blood pressure 126/68 mm Hg, pulse 90/min, respiratory rate 14/min, and temperature 99.4 °F. Abdominal exam revealed distension tenderness to Mcburney’s point, with guarding and rigidity but bowel sounds were normal. Laboratory data showed hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit 40.1%, white count 8.1 × 103/µL, platelets 172 × 103/µL, sodium 133 mmol/L, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, chloride 99 mmol/L, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, BUN 24 mg/dL, creatinine 2.1 mg/dL and glucose 181 mg/dL. Computed tomography (CT) scan of abdomen showed the perforated appendicitis with two appendicoliths seen in the right lower quadrant (). Laparoscopic appendectomy with drainage of periappendiceal abscess was performed. Surgical pathology of appendix showed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with signet-ring cell (< 50% signet ring component) and carcinoid features involving muscular wall (). Tumor was penetrating and perforating the visceral serosa and diffusely infiltrating > 4/5 wall of the proximal portion of appendix with lymphovascular invasion. Special studies showed that the tumor is immunoreactive to CK20 (), focal to CD56, chromogranin (), synaptophysin; negative to CK7 (), TTF-1, PSA and CDx2. Mucin stain is positive (). The immunostains support the diagnosis. Patient was started on chemotherapy and after 3 months, patient complained of abdominal pain for which CT abdomen was performed which showed findings suspicious for spreading cancer. Patient failed chemotherapy and underwent hemicolectomy and peritonectomy as there were seedings on the peritoneum.

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)
bicarbonate: Serum bicarbonate in mEq/L (e.g., 18)

def extract_calculator_values(
    patient_note: str,
    calculator_name: str,
    field_descriptions: str,
    reasoning_context: str,
) -> dict:
    """Extract specific clinical values from a patient note for a medical calculator.

    You are given:
    - A patient note with clinical information

    - For sex: "man"/"male"/"he" → "male", "woman"/"female"/"she" → "female"
    - For boolean conditions: True if present, False if absent
    - If a condition is not mentioned, assume it is absent (False or 0)
    - Include ALL fields, even if the value is False/0/absent

    Return a dict with:
    - "extracted": {"param_name": value, ...} for each field
    - "missing": ["list of values truly not determinable from the note"]

    Examples:
    >>> extract_calculator_values("A 70yo male, 80kg, 175cm, Cr 1.2", "Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault)", "age: years, sex: male/female, weight_kg: kg, creatinine_mg_dl: mg/dL", "")
    {'extracted': {'age': 70, 'sex': 'male', 'weight_kg': 80, 'creatinine_mg_dl': 1.2}, 'missing': []}
    """
    ...

Patient was examined and investigated thoroughly. Temperature was 36.3 C, blood pressure was 138/81, and pulse was 78. Physical examination revealed a tender, firm and distended abdomen with hyperactive sounds. Laboratory analysis revealed hemoglobin at 10.3 g/dL, leukocytes 3.1 K/cmm, urea nitrogen 19 mg/dl, creatinine 0.6 mg/dl, sodium 137 mmol/l, potassium 3.6 mmol/l, chloride 104 mmol/l, bicarbonate 24 mmol/l, calcium 8.6 mg/dl, total protein 6.3 g/dl, albumin 3.1 g/dl, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dl, alkaline phosphatase 98 U/l, SGOT 20 U/l, SGPT 27 U/l. Laboratory values were consistent with patient’s previous clinic visits. Computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen revealed a fluid-filled, significantly dilated stomach as well as multiple dilated loops of small bowel with air-fluid levels consistent with mechanical small bowel obstruction caused by intussusception of small bowel (Fig. ). After initial fluid resuscitation and antibiotic administration, consent was obtained for emergent exploratory laparotomy. The exploratory laparotomy revealed a 3.0 × 2.5 cm intraluminal calculus in the ileum responsible for the small bowel obstruction, without evidence of intussusception (Fig. ). The obstructing enterolith was ‘milked’ proximally until it reached, and subsequently ‘fell into,’ an isolated 6.0 × 3.0 cm jejunal diverticulum which had not been previously noted on CT imaging. The enterolith was removed along with the segment of the jejunum containing the isolated diverticulum, which was the source of the enterolith (Fig. ). On histologic examination, the mucosa of the extracted diverticulum was found to be granular and erythematous; the remaining small bowel had marked mucosal ulceration and near-transmural inflammation with granulation tissue. Hospital course was complicated by decreased urine output that resolved after fluid resuscitation. On the fourth postoperative day, nasogastric tube was discontinued, diet was advanced and tolerated, and the patient was discharged. Review of the patient’s CT scan of the abdomen revealed the isolated jejunal diverticulum that was consistent with the excised diverticulum (Fig. ).

args:
A 33-year-old man, without known co-morbidities, was brought into the emergency department with sudden onset of headache and severe central chest pain, radiating to the back. The patient arrived 3.5 hours after the onset of symptoms and was still in severe pain.
The blood pressure was 180/125 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats per minute (bpm), temperature 36.1°C, oxygen-saturation 98% and the respiratory-rate was 28 per minute. An arterial blood gas (ABG) revealed significant lactate acidosis with pH 7.14, pO2 of 15.4 kPa, pCO2 of 4.5 kPa, bicarbonate of 12 mEq/L, base excess of −16 mmol/L and lactate value of 12 mmol/L. A 12 lead electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm with slight ST-depressions in the leads V4–6. 
Bedside echocardiography revealed regional hypokinesia in the lateral area of the left ventricle, and an estimated left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 20–30%.
The patients’ blood samples demonstrated abnormal with a troponin T of 300 ng/L, white blood cell count of 39 × 109/L and normal c-reactive protein.
An immediate thoracic/abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan was performed, testing negative for aortic dissection or large pulmonary embolism. However, a 6 cm large tumor in the left adrenal gland was found in .
During the first hours of observation, the patient’s symptoms spontaneously resolved. His vitals normalized and a repeated ABG showed a rising pH (7.33) and decreasing lactate (5.6 mmol/L). 
Meanwhile, the patient’s previous history was explored in more detail. The evening before his admission, he had consumed multiple energy drinks, but denies the intake of alcohol or any other substances. 
Nine years earlier, he presented with similar symptoms at another hospital, likewise had spontaneously recovered and was quickly discharged without a firm diagnosis. Similarly, 4 years prior to this admission, the patient was acutely hospitalized with headache, back pain, nausea, elevated lactate, white blood cell count and blood pressure. That admission summary described that all symptoms remitted, his vitals as well as blood values normalized over a few hours and the patient was discharged after a day of observation, without finding a plausible explanation to his symptoms. 
Due to these findings and the suspicion of pheochromocytoma-induced cardiomyopathy, the patient was admitted. During the 7-day admission, the patient’s vitals remained normal and blood samples including troponin T slowly normalized. 
A repeated transthoracic echocardiography showed a LVEF of 45% in .
Plasma cortisol, renin, aldosterone and a 24-hour urine sample for steroid metabolites were within normal range. 3-Metthoxyadrenalin was elevated to 2,5 nmol/L (normal range: < 0,46 nmol/L) as well as 3-Metthoxynoradrenalin at 29,6 nmol/L (normal range < 1,09 nmol/L). 
During this observation, the patient reported no symptoms and was discharged for planned surgery in an outpatient setting. 
Due to the size of the tumor (6 cm in diameter) and hence a slightly increased risk of malignancy, open left-sided adrenalectomy was performed []. 
The patient was discharged on the fifth postoperative day after uncomplicated surgery and observation period. Follow-up showed normalization of 3-metthoxyadrenalin and 3-metthoxynoradrenalin, and the patient had not experienced any symptoms or complications. 
The left adrenal gland was found with a well-defined pheochromocytoma with a maximum diameter of 25 mm. The tumor morphology was with the growth of large nests and solid areas, high cellularity and areas with spindle cells as well as areas with profound nuclear pleomorphism and nuclear hyperchromasia. Thus, the Pheochromocytoma of Adrenal Gland Scales (PASS) score was 8 in []. It is not possible to predict the malign potential of a pheochromocytoma from the histopathology, but a PASS score > 4 indicates potential for aggressive clinical behavior. Thus, the patient will be followed with annually blood tests (3-metthoxyadrenalin, 3-metthoxynoradrenalin) lifelong. [].

output.reasoning: Patient is a 33-year-old man. HEART Score components: History (suspected ACS with chest pain, but atypical with spontaneous resolution and history of similar episodes, plus pheochromocytoma diagnosis, so moderate risk history = 2pts). Age: 33 (<45 = 0pts). Risk factors: hypertension (BP 180/125, and implied by pheochromocytoma), but no smoking, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, family history, or obesity mentioned (1 risk factor = 1pt). ECG: slight ST-depressions (abnormal but not ischemic = 1pt). Troponin: elevated (300 ng/L, above normal = 2pts). Total HEART Score = 6 (History=2, Age=0, Risk=1, ECG=1, Troponin=2). Conditions present: history_suspected_acs (moderate risk), hypertension, abnormal_ecg, elevated_troponin. Conditions absent: age_45_above, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking, family_history_acs, obesity. Demographics: age 33, sex male.

An immediate thoracic/abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan was performed, testing negative for aortic dissection or large pulmonary embolism. However, a 6 cm large tumor in the left adrenal gland was found in .
During the first hours of observation, the patient’s symptoms spontaneously resolved. His vitals normalized and a repeated ABG showed a rising pH (7.33) and decreasing lactate (5.6 mmol/L). 
Meanwhile, the patient’s previous history was explored in more detail. The evening before his admission, he had consumed multiple energy drinks, but denies the intake of alcohol or any other substances. 
Nine years earlier, he presented with similar symptoms at another hospital, likewise had spontaneously recovered and was quickly discharged without a firm diagnosis. Similarly, 4 years prior to this admission, the patient was acutely hospitalized with headache, back pain, nausea, elevated lactate, white blood cell count and blood pressure. That admission summary described that all symptoms remitted, his vitals as well as blood values normalized over a few hours and the patient was discharged after a day of observation, without finding a plausible explanation to his symptoms. 
Due to these findings and the suspicion of pheochromocytoma-induced cardiomyopathy, the patient was admitted. During the 7-day admission, the patient’s vitals remained normal and blood samples including troponin T slowly normalized. 
A repeated transthoracic echocardiography showed a LVEF of 45% in .
Plasma cortisol, renin, aldosterone and a 24-hour urine sample for steroid metabolites were within normal range. 3-Metthoxyadrenalin was elevated to 2,5 nmol/L (normal range: < 0,46 nmol/L) as well as 3-Metthoxynoradrenalin at 29,6 nmol/L (normal range < 1,09 nmol/L). 
During this observation, the patient reported no symptoms and was discharged for planned surgery in an outpatient setting. 
Due to the size of the tumor (6 cm in diameter) and hence a slightly increased risk of malignancy, open left-sided adrenalectomy was performed []. 
The patient was discharged on the fifth postoperative day after uncomplicated surgery and observation period. Follow-up showed normalization of 3-metthoxyadrenalin and 3-metthoxynoradrenalin, and the patient had not experienced any symptoms or complications. 
The left adrenal gland was found with a well-defined pheochromocytoma with a maximum diameter of 25 mm. The tumor morphology was with the growth of large nests and solid areas, high cellularity and areas with spindle cells as well as areas with profound nuclear pleomorphism and nuclear hyperchromasia. Thus, the Pheochromocytoma of Adrenal Gland Scales (PASS) score was 8 in []. It is not possible to predict the malign potential of a pheochromocytoma from the histopathology, but a PASS score > 4 indicates potential for aggressive clinical behavior. Thus, the patient will be followed with annually blood tests (3-metthoxyadrenalin, 3-metthoxynoradrenalin) lifelong. [].

args: A 48-year-old South Asian woman (Patient A) from Bangladesh (height 168 cm, weight 80 kg) was admitted to the medical ward for three days after which she was transferred to the ICU; she had cough and increasing dyspnea for three days before hospital admission. Comorbidities included hypertension, hypercholesterinemia, coronary artery disease, previous stroke (fully recovered), and type 2 diabetes. On admission, her laboratory results were as follows: hemoglobin (Hb): 143 g/L, white blood cells (WBC): 9.0 × 109/L, lymphocytes: 1.4 × 109/L, platelets: 261 × 109/L, international normalized ratio (INR): 1.1, D-dimer: 510 μg/L, fibrinogen plasma concentration: 8.4 g/L, and C-reactive protein (CRP): 52 mg/L. Her chest radiograph showed cardiomegaly and extensive bilateral peripherally predominant ground-glass opacities. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) result was positive for severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) and negative for influence/respiratory syncytial virus (RSV). Blood cultures were negative. The patient was treated with nasal oxygen therapy and antibiotics (ceftriaxone and doxycycline, according the hospital’s COVID-19 protocol). Antiviral therapy and dexamethasone were not administered. Later, CRP increased to 137 mg/L on the second day of hospitalization. The patient was transferred to the ICU on the third day because of increased respiratory rate and oxygen requirement. ROTEM performed 2 hours after ICU admission () revealed that Patient A was hypercoagulable with the EXTEM assay showing an increased clot firmness with an amplitude of clot firmness 5 minutes after coagulation time (CT) (A5) of 65 mm and a maximum clot firmness (MCF) of 78 mm, indicating hypercoagulability with a high risk of thrombosis []. FIBTEM also showed increased clot firmness (A5 41 mm and MCF 50 mm), indicating increased fibrinogen concentration and fibrin polymerization. Furthermore, the EXTEM lysis index 60 minutes after CT (LI60) was 97%, i.e., in the physiologic range (82–97.9%), whereas FIBTEM LI60 was 100% []. Treatment consisted of continuation of antibiotics, enoxaparin 40 mg twice a day in view of ROTEM results, and high flow nasal oxygen and intermittent face mask continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapies. The patient did not need intermittent positive pressure ventilation (IPPV) or any vasoactive support. Laboratory results on the second day in the ICU were as follows: Hb: 131 g/L, WBC: 10.8 × 109/L, platelet count: 307 × 109/L, INR: 1.1, activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) ratio: 1.3, D-dimer: 510 μg/L, and CRP: 196 mg/L. The patient recovered well and was transferred from the ICU to the medical ward after two days in the ICU with a CRP of 73 mg/L and was discharged three days later with a prescription of her usual medication plus enoxaparin 40 mg once daily for 2 weeks. The patient did not show any clinical signs of thrombosis during her hospital stay.

weight_kg: Actual patient weight in kilograms (e.g., 100.0)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
height_cm: Patient height in centimeters (e.g., 170.0)

Consider the following documentation stub for a Python function.  Note
that this is documentation, not a full implementation.
```python
def simulate_medical_value(patient_note: str, question: str) -> float:
    """Calculate a medical value from a patient note.

    Calculate a medical value from a patient note.
    
    Given a patient note and a calculation question, reason step by step:
    1. Carefully read the patient note to extract all relevant clinical values
    2. Identify what medical calculation/score is needed
    3. Apply the appropriate formula from the reference below
    4. Show your calculation step by step, checking your arithmetic
    
    FORMULAS (use these exact formulas):
    - APACHE II Score: Acute Physiology Score + Age Points + Chronic Health Points
    - Adjusted Body Weight: ABW = IBW + 0.4 * (actual_weight - IBW)
    - Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    - Anion Gap: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    - Body Mass Index (BMI): BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2
    - Body Surface Area (Mosteller): BSA = sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600)
    - CHA2DS2-VASc Score: Sum of: CHF(+1), HTN(+1), Age>=75(+2)/65-74(+1), DM(+1), Stroke/TIA(+2), Vascular(+1), Female(+1)
    - CKD-EPI GFR (2021): 142 * min(Cr/kappa,1)^alpha * max(Cr/kappa,1)^-1.200 * 0.9938^age * sex_coef
    - CURB-65 Score: C + U + R + B + 65 (0-5 points)
    - Calcium Correction for Hypoalbuminemia: Corrected Ca = Ca + 0.8 * (4 - albumin)
    - Caprini VTE Score: Sum of weighted risk factors
    - Centor Score (McIsaac): Sum with age modifier
    - Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI): Sum of weighted comorbidity points + age points
    - Child-Pugh Score: Sum of points for bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
    - Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault): CrCl = ((140 - age) * adjusted_weight * gender_coef) / (Cr * 72)
    - Date of Conception: gestational_weeks - 2 (conception ~2 weeks after LMP)
    - Delta Gap: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    - Delta Ratio: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    - Estimated Due Date: 40 - current_gestational_age
    - FIB-4 Index: FIB-4 = (Age * AST) / (Platelets * sqrt(ALT))
    - FeverPAIN Score: Fever + Purulence + Attend rapidly + Inflamed tonsils + No cough/coryza (0-5)
    - Fractional Excretion of Sodium (FENa): FENa = (UNa * SCr) / (SNa * UCr) * 100
    - Framingham Risk Score: Complex multivariate equation based on age, sex, lipids, BP, smoking
    - Free Water Deficit: FWD = TBW% * weight * (Na/140 - 1)
    - Gestational Age: weeks + days/7
    - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): Eye + Verbal + Motor (3-15)
    - Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): Sum of clinical and lab criteria (0-23)
    - HAS-BLED Score: H-A-S-B-L-E-D criteria (0-9 points)
    - HEART Score: Sum of History + ECG + Age + Risk factors + Troponin (each 0-2 points)
    - HOMA-IR: HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405
    - Ideal Body Weight (Devine): Male: IBW = 50 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60), Female: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
    - LDL Calculated (Friedewald): LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides / 5)
    - MDRD GFR: 175 * (Cr)^-1.154 * (age)^-0.203 * sex_coef
    - MELD-Na Score: MELD = 10 * (0.957*ln(Cr) + 0.378*ln(Bili) + 1.120*ln(INR) + 0.643)
    - Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule): First 10kg: 4ml/kg/hr, Next 10kg: 2ml/kg/hr, Remaining: 1ml/kg/hr
    - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): MAP = (2 * DBP + SBP) / 3
    - Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME): Sum of (opioid dose * conversion factor)
    - PERC Rule: 8 criteria; PERC negative if all 0
    - PSI/PORT Score: Demographics + Comorbidities + Physical Exam + Labs
    - QTc (Bazett): QTc = QT / sqrt(RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Framingham): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Fridericia): QTc = QT / RR^(1/3) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Hodges): QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)
    - QTc (Rautaharju): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): Sum of 6 risk factors (0-6 points)
    - SIRS Criteria: Count of criteria met (>= 2 = SIRS positive)
    - SOFA Score: Sum of 6 organ system scores (0-24)

A 41-year-old female, with a past medical history of DM1 treated with insulin glargine and insulin aspart, presented with a 1-day history of diffuse abdominal pain and weakness. She admitted to abstaining from any solid food intake in the previous 2 days; however, she maintained her liquid intake and continued taking her insulin glargine without insulin aspart. She denied any alcohol intake or drug abuse. Her vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination was significant for diffuse abdominal tenderness without rigidity or guarding and a ruptured Bartholin's gland abscess with drainage to the skin. Blood work showed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 13,000 cells/mcL (normal range: 4,500-10,000/mcL), hemoglobin level 11.2 mg/dL (normal range: 12.3-15.3 g/dL), platelet count 448,000/mcL (normal range: 150,000-450,000/mcL), sodium 135 mmol/L (normal range: 136-145 mmol/L), potassium 3.6 mmol/L (normal range: 3.5-5.1 mmol/L), chloride 105 mmol/L (normal range: 98-107 mmol/L), bicarbonate 6 mmol/L (normal range: 21-32 mmol/L),  urea nitrogen 4 mg/dL (normal range: 7-18 mg/dL), creatinine 0.58 mg/dL (normal range: 0.6-1.3 mg/dL), glucose 191 mg/dL (normal range: 70-100 mg/dL), beta hydroxybutyrate >4.5 mg/dL (normal range: <0.4 mg/dL), magnesium 1.3 mg/dL (normal range: 1.6-2.3 mg/dL), phosphorus 1.3 mg/dL (normal range: 2.5-4.9 mg/dL), lactate 0.8 mmol/L (normal range: 0.4-2 mmol/L), and HBA1C 12.5% (normal range: 4-5.6%). Urinalysis was significant for +3 ketones and sugar. Arterial blood gas showed pH 7.07, pCO2 12. A urine drug screen was negative, and serum ethanol level was undetectable.
The patient was diagnosed with euglycemic DKA precipitated by an underlying infection. Intravenous fluid (IVF) resuscitation with normal saline was initially started at a rate of 1.0 L/h for a total of 2 L. The patient's hydration status, serum electrolytes, blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine, glucose, and urine output were monitored closely. After 2 h, repeat blood work showed a serum sodium 141 mmol/L, carbon dioxide 8 mmol/L, potassium 3.5 mmol/L, and glucose 153 mg/dL. Subsequently, IVFs were switched to 5% dextrose and 0.45% NaCl at a rate of 250 cc/h, and an insulin drip at a rate of 5 units/h was started. Then, 30 mEq potassium was added to each liter of the IVF, excluding the first liter, targeting a serum potassium level of 4-5 mEq/L. After 11 h, blood work revealed a serum bicarbonate level of 16 mmol/L with a normal anion gap; hence, subcutaneous insulin and oral intake were initiated. Intravenous insulin was continued for another hour to ensure adequate plasma insulin levels. The Bartholin's gland abscess was treated, and the patient was discharged on the following day.

chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)

The adjusted body weight is calculated using the formula: Adjusted weight = ideal body weight + 0.5 * (actual body weight - ideal body weight). The ideal body weight for women is 45.5 kg + 2.3 kg for each inch over 5 feet. The patient is 5 feet 4 inches tall (64 inches), so ideal body weight = 45.5 + 2.3 * 4 = 45.5 + 9.2 = 54.7 kg. Actual weight is 89 kg. Adjusted body weight = 54.7 + 0.5 * (89 - 54.7) = 54.7 + 0.5 * 34.3 = 54.7 + 17.15 = 71.85 kg.

71.85
</answer>

args: A 38-year-old postpartum woman arrived to the ambulatory triage area late this afternoon because her left lower leg had become noticeably heavier and mildly painful over the preceding 24 hours. Six weeks ago she delivered her second child via uncomplicated scheduled cesarean section that required spinal anesthesia and an overnight stay; she was discharged on postoperative day 2 and has since resumed routine household activities without assistive devices. Yesterday morning, while standing at the kitchen counter for an extended period preparing bottles, she felt a dull pressure deep in the posterior aspect of the calf that worsened when she bore weight and eased modestly with elevation. By evening she observed that her sock left an imprint on that side but not on the right. She denies antecedent trauma, ankle sprain, insect bite, recent intense exercise, or local skin infection. She also denies fevers, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, or constitutional complaints. There is no history of malignant disease, hematologic disorder, or prior venous clot, nor has she ever required anticoagulation. She reports no neurologic illness, limb weakness, or application of a plaster cast, and she has remained fully mobile caring for her newborn. She uses no estrogen-containing contraception and takes only prenatal vitamins and 600 mg ibuprofen as needed for incisional soreness. Both parents are alive and free of venous thromboembolic events; no siblings have clotting disorders. She has never smoked, drinks wine rarely, and denies illicit drug use. On presentation she appeared comfortable holding the infant carrier, spoke in complete sentences, and ambulated without assistance. Vital signs before any medications were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rhythm with no murmurs; lungs were clear to auscultation. Abdominal inspection showed a well-healed Pfannenstiel scar that was nontender with no palpable masses or organomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated subtle fullness confined to the left calf; there was no erythema, hyperpigmentation, varicosity, or brawny skin change. A flexible tape placed 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity measured 38.0 cm on the left and 36.4 cm on the right, a disparity of 1.6 cm. Circumference at mid-thigh and around the ankle was symmetric. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia and dorsum of the left foot produced a 1+ indentation that resolved within 15 seconds, whereas identical pressure on the right left no depression. Palpation along the course of the popliteal vein and posteromedial calf elicited focal discomfort described by the patient as “a deep bruise,” compared with no tenderness over the contralateral limb; squeezing the gastrocnemius muscle belly away from the neurovascular bundle produced only minimal diffuse soreness. There was no palpable subcutaneous cord and no crepitus. Passive dorsiflexion of the ankle did not reproduce pain at the Achilles insertion. Skin temperature was marginally warmer over the symptomatic calf compared with the right when assessed with the dorsum of the examiner’s hand. Distal pulses—dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial—were 2+ bilaterally, and capillary refill of all toes was under two seconds. Motor strength in ankle dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, and great-toe extension was 5/5, and sensation to light touch and vibration was intact. Straight-leg raise and seated slump test were negative for radicular symptoms, making a spinal etiology improbable. There was no ipsilateral knee effusion, meniscal tenderness, or ligamentous laxity, and range of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle was full. No inguinal lymphadenopathy was appreciated. Basic laboratory studies obtained prior to any antithrombotic therapy showed hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, platelet count 248 × 10⁹/L, white blood cells 7.1 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and an INR of 1.0 with activated partial thromboplastin time 29 seconds. These values reveal no anemia, thrombocytopenia, or renal impairment that might confound subsequent management. Given the recent abdominal-pelvic surgical intervention within the past two months, the presence of unilateral pitting edema confined to the calf, focal tenderness along the deep venous pathway, and the absence of a compelling musculoskeletal, dermatologic, or neurologic explanation, an acute thrombotic obstruction of the deep venous system remains the leading consideration at this stage of evaluation.

Paralysis/paresis? No, motor strength intact, denies limb weakness. Recently bedridden >3 days? No, discharged POD2 and resumed routine activities. Localized tenderness along deep venous system? Yes, focal discomfort along popliteal vein and posteromedial calf. Entire leg swollen? No, swelling confined to left calf (1.6cm asymmetry). Calf swelling >3cm? Yes, 1.6cm difference (criteria requires >3cm, so absent). Pitting edema? Yes, 1+ indentation on left. Collateral superficial veins? No, no varicosity mentioned. Alternative diagnosis as likely? Conditions absent: active_cancer, paralysis_paresis, recently_bedridden, entire_leg_swollen, calf_swelling_3cm, collateral_superficial_veins, alternative_diagnosis.

args: A 62-year-old female with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presented to the emergency department this morning complaining of a dull ache behind the right knee that began yesterday afternoon while she was attempting to ambulate from her bedroom to the bathroom. Nine days ago she was discharged from an outside hospital after an elective right hemicolectomy for complicated diverticulitis; during that admission she remained largely supine for the first four postoperative days, and since returning home she reports spending most hours in bed because of fatigue and abdominal discomfort, rising only for meals and toileting. She denies any history of malignancy, clotting disorder, or prior vein thrombosis, and she has not required a cast, brace, or splint on either lower extremity. Today she noted that her right ankle and lower calf appeared somewhat puffier than usual and that the skin over that region left an indentation when she pressed with her thumb, yet she did not observe comparable changes in the opposite limb. She specifically denies redness, warmth, or diffuse swelling from groin to foot, and she has not experienced sudden shortness of breath, chest pain, or palpitations. She denies recent trauma, fever, chills, weight loss, night sweats, or urinary complaints. Current medications include enteric-coated aspirin 81 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and postoperative oxycodone 5 mg taken sparingly for incisional pain; she is not on hormone replacement, oral contraceptives, or anticoagulation. Vital signs on arrival: temperature 98.6 °F, heart rate 86 bpm, blood pressure 138/78 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air. She appears tired but in no acute distress, speaking full sentences without dyspnea. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable with regular S1/S2, no murmurs, clear bilateral breath sounds, and no jugular venous distention. Abdominal surgical incision is clean, dry, and intact with mild perincisional tenderness expected for postoperative day 9, and no abdominal distention or guarding. Musculoskeletal and vascular examination of the legs reveals normal skin temperature bilaterally and symmetric palpable posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulses. The right lower calf and ankle demonstrate 1+ pitting edema that is not present on the left side; tape-measure circumference at 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity shows a difference of 1.2 cm. There is no cord-like induration, varicosities, or engorged superficial channels over either calf or thigh. Gentle palpation over the right femoral triangle, popliteal fossa, posterior calf, and along the course of the peroneal veins elicits no focal tenderness. Range of motion at the right knee and ankle is full and pain free except for the vague ache she describes deep in the posterior knee while standing. Sensation is intact and motor strength is 5/5 in all major muscle groups; there is no weakness, foot drop, or spasticity. Neurologic examination is otherwise nonfocal. Laboratory studies drawn in triage demonstrate hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, platelet count 235 × 10^3/µL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, INR 1.0; comprehensive metabolic panel is within normal limits. Given her recent major abdominal surgery less than two weeks prior, the prolonged time confined to bed greater than three consecutive days, and the new unilateral dependent edema, venous thromboembolic disease is high on the working differential. args: A fifty-five-year-old Afghanistan gentleman with a history of hyperthyroidism (5 years) and ischemic cardiomyopathy (2 years) presented to Aga Khan University Hospital in July 2012. He was found to have kidney failure 2 months previously and was initiated on hemodialysis due to uremic encephalopathy. He was admitted to our institution for further work-up investigations for his kidney failure. His laboratory investigations were as follows:hemoglobin: 9 g/dL, white cell counts: 4,700 u/L, platelet count: 171,000/mm3. Urine DR showed yellow color with clear appearance, pH of 5, specific gravity of 1.010, 4 red blood cells per high-power field, 1 WBCs per high-power field, urine protein of 1.0 g/L, and 2 RBCs cast andurine protein to creatinine ratio: 3.9, BUN: 109 mg/dL, creatinine: 4.8 mg/dL, electrolytes: serum sodium 141 mmol/L, potassium 5.0 mmol/L, chloride 116 mmol/L, and bicarbonate 14.5 mmol/L, ANA: negative, C-ANCA: negative, P-ANCA: negative, hepatitis B surface antigen: nonreactive, hepatitis C antibody: nonreactive, PT/APTT/INR: 10.5/30.2/1. Because of symptomatic uremia, a right internal jugular nontunneled catheter was inserted on 28 of July 2012 for hemodialysis. In view of his normal size kidneys, a biopsy was done on 1 August 2012 to look for reversible causes. The biopsy showed 20 glomeruli in which 14 were globally sclerosed or severely collapsed and condensed. Several of them were associated with fibrocellular crescents with disruption of bowman's capsule and 4 had cellular crescents. Immunofluorescence showed IgA positivity in the mesangium and along the capillary walls. He was pulsed with methyl prednisolone 500 mg once a day for 3 days followed by oral prednisolone 25 mg twice a day. Cyclophosphamide or mycophenolate mofetil was not added in view of 14/20 sclerosed glomeruli and fibrocellular crescents. As there was no sign of renal recovery, the patient was discharged on three times a week dialysis through right internal jugular catheter with followup in nephrology outpatients clinic.

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)
bicarbonate: Serum bicarbonate in mEq/L (e.g., 18)

calculator_name: CKD-EPI Equations for Glomerular Filtration Rate

args: Using the 2021 CKD-EPI Creatinine equation, what is the patient's Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) in terms of mL/min/1.73 m²? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

output.reasoning: Question specifically mentions 2021 CKD-EPI Creatinine equation for calculating GFR

args:
A 53-year-old Hispanic male with poorly controlled diabetes, hemodialysis for two years, and coronary artery bypass grafting 10 months previously, developed redness pain and swelling of the medial aspect of both legs two days prior to admission. This occurred in a symmetrical fashion and extended from the upper one-third of the calf to the mid-thigh. The patient had difficulty walking secondary to pain. He had undergone saphenous vein harvesting from these areas for his coronary artery bypass graft (CABG). The patient denied trauma to the lower extremities, fevers, chills, and rigors. His medications include paricalcitol, sevelamer, calcium acetate, aspirin, amiodarone, amlodepine, simvastatin, and humilin 70/30.
Physical examination showed extensive erythema involving the inner aspect of the thighs down to the medial upper one-third of the calf on both sides (Figures -). Skin lesions were warm and very tender to touch, but there was no induration. Initial laboratory examination revealed white count 9,500 with 70% neutrophils, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 87 mm/hr, C-reactive protein 11.6 mg/dl, blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 57 mg/dl, creatinine 11.3 mg/dl, calcium 9.8 mg/dl, albumin 3 gm/dl, prothrombin time (PT) 11.3 sec, international normalized ratio (INR) 1.1, and activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) 30.3 sec.
He was treated with intravenous vancomycin and ceftriaxone. By day three, moderate induration had developed, there were no signs of improvement, and the intensity of pain worsened. CT scan of both lower extremities was not consistent with necrotizing fasciitis and repeat labs showed a white count 15,700, with 75% neutrophils, and C-reactive protein 48 mg/dl, calcium 9.76 mg/dl, phosphorus 12.5 mg/dl (first time obtained). He required a patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) pump to control his pain with little improvement. His antibiotics were changed to linezolid, tobramycin, and levofloxacin. On the fourth day, there was an increased induration of the thighs with rock hard consistency of the tissue. Skin biopsy was performed showing deposition of calcium in the small blood vessels of the skin, areas of fat necrosis with foamy histiocytes, and neutrophils consistent with calciphylaxis (Figures -). Parathyroid hormone level (PTH) was 628 pg/ml.
He received daily dialysis with intravenous sodium thiosulfate given post dialysis for three days. His calcium-containing phosphate binders and paricalcitol were stopped; dosage was increased for sevelamer and lanthanum carbonate, another phosphate binder, was added to lower phosphorus levels. The patient’s pain, redness, swelling and induration improved over the next three days and his calcium was 8.5 gm/dl and phosphorus 7.3 mg/dl at the time of discharge.

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
creatinine_mg_dl: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.2)

A 67-year-old male with a past medical history of untreated hepatitis C and hypertension was admitted after experiencing nausea, lightheadedness and a syncopal episode resulting in trauma to his head. En route to the hospital, the patient had one episode of hematemesis, followed by two additional episodes in the emergency department. Vital signs were as follows: heart rate 100 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, blood pressure 131/71 mmHg and temperature 94.3°F. The patient was oriented to person, place and time. His abdomen was soft, non-distended and non-tender to palpation. Pertinent laboratory tests included hemoglobin 9.5 g/dl, hematocrit 29.5%, platelets 197 K/µL, blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 22 mg/dl, creatinine 0.8 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 112 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 76 U/L, total bilirubin 0.5 mg/dl, alkaline phosphatase 18 U/L, albumin 3.2 g/dl, lactic acid 3.1 mmol/L, prothrombin time 11.8 seconds and international normalized ratio (INR) 1.0.
